speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Number one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want both of us together? You want both of us together?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morn. Today's January 6, 1997. My name is Shulamit Bastacky. I'm interviewing survivor Malka, last name at birth, Klin. And last name is Baran. Interview is conducted in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, United States. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me your first and your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Malka, M-A-L-K-A, Baran, B-A-R-A-N. Former Klin, K-L-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city and what country were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Warsaw. Spell it? W-A-R-S-A-W, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born January 30th, 1927, in Warsaw. And when I was one year old, we moved to Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old does it make you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm now 70. I will be 70 this month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, can you tell us about your very early childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were born in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Warsaw. My mother's family lived in Warsaw, my grandfather, grandmother, and three- two sisters and three brothers. My father was from Czestochowa. After I was born, a year after, we moved to Czestochowa. And there, I spent my childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your, uh, parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Izak Klin and Bella Kotz Klin. And we lived in a very small apartment, one bedroom and a kitchen in an apartment house. Uh, my father had a printer shop in the same building. And two years later, my brother was born. So we were a family of four, a small family, with a very large extensive family in Czestochowa and Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Warsaw located in Poland? What part of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I would say it's in middle Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Czestochowa, what part of Poland is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not far from Warsaw. It's-- I'm not good at geography, but I think it's-- uh-- I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: You grew up in Czestochowa. Can you tell me what-- about your house, what kind of house you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in an apartment house, as I said, a small apartment. And I attended school in Czestochowa, a private school. We were just like a normal middle class, lower middle class, I would say, family. I attended a very, um, special school because my mother's sister was a secretary there. So we sort of got a break. And I had a fairly happy childhood. I was interested in studies. I was fairly good at school. I had friends in the neighborhood. Uh, I did my homework. We played outside. We celebrated certain holidays in a certain way. And walked to stores, through shops, walked on avenues.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember incidents that were sort of funny and pleasant. I remember a winter day, walking with my friend in the avenue in Czestochowa. The snow was falling. Snowflakes were falling. And we were playing games with people that we met. I used to stop and say, excuse me. What time is it? And the lady or gentleman would kindly answer. And then we went a few steps later. And my girlfriend's turn it was. And she stopped another person, what time is it? And those were sort of innocent games of childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, uh, getting up in the morning very early before school. Our school was located at the end of the city. So it took a good, I would say, 40 minutes to walk. We were not driven to school. We didn't have a car. We walked in the winters and all other seasons.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably 40 minutes, but we got up very early. My friends whistled downstairs. And we got together. We walked to a garden, a public garden. And I remember sitting there before school and reading poetry with my friends, sort of like that. And then we came to school at about 8:00, 8:30. And we started our classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to your apartment, how big of an apartment was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: One room, one, uh, living room, which also was a bedroom and a kitchen, a small apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there a kitchen? Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: A very small kitchen. Uh, the bathrooms were downstairs in the courtyard. Um, and there was no-- we had electricity. We had a very primitive telephone from the apartment to my father's print shop. That's all, sort of a hand telephone. And we heard him calling us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the apartment building?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a center courtyard around which there were apartments with little verandas to the courtyard. And maybe-- I would say maybe 15 or 12 tenants, something like that, around the building.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was one-bedroom apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: One-bedroom apartment. One bedroom and a kitchen, that's all. There was no living room. The bedroom and the living room were one unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did you do in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my favorite occupation was reading. I loved reading books. I played with the children outside. I didn't have many toys. Almost-- I don't remember any toys at all. Uh, walking with my father to the city, uh, on Saturdays, and, uh, helping him later when I was a little older in the print shop. I was interested in what he was doing. Going to the movies. My father printed tickets for the movie houses. So we had a free-- uh, free entrance to the movies. And it was interesting. We had a girl who was non-Jewish. And she helped my mother. And she walked-- Czestochowa had a shrine, a very important church, where there-- Mar-- Mary's picture was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What religion--

speaker: interviewee
text: And pilgrims-- Christian, Christian, Catholic. And pilgrims came from all over Poland and were walking-- Czestochowa was built like a long, long avenue from which other streets were coming from. And the pilgrims were walking up to that holy place on a little mountain. And I was watching them on the street. And my-- this girl who worked for us, with us, used to go there on Sundays. And my brother and I used to go with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the-- this girl that was working for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: The area where you lived, was it a Jewish neighborhood, Jewish area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, very Jewish. In fact, it was part of the ghetto later.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big of an area was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] That, I cannot tell you. I was very young. And I'm very ignorant in areas and, uh, number of people. I know that Czestochowa had about 42,000 Jews. I know that from later. And only 2,000 were, uh, left in the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- did you observe any holidays or-- at your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were for-- assimilated. We did-- my father didn't go to synagogue every Shabbas, only once a year. And my mother lit candles on Friday night. But beside that, we had a non-Kosher home. Sep-- dishes were not separated. On Passover, we had some semblance of matzoh but also ate bread. So it was fairly assim-- very assimilated home. I didn't know much about religion. But I knew I had a very-- and I still have a very private connection with God somehow. My parents did not teach me. Every night before I went to sleep, I used to say words to God privately. I put my arm, as a child, on my heart. And I said good night to him. And thank him for the-- for my brother, for my parents, and ask him to keep them well. And then I did not open my eyes anymore, even if I didn't sleep. It was a good-bye to a special person. And that was my relationship, which remained throughout the years. So I don't know where it came from, but I am very-- now, I understand. I'm very interested in the spiritual-- spirituality. So that probably comes from within me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a brother. He was murdered with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was two years younger. In fact, in 1949-- 1939, when the Germans came and occupied Czestochowa, he was bar mitzvahed. He was studying to become bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: So, uh, how did you get along with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty well, although he was what we call in Hebrew a [HEBREW], a little mischief-maker. So I always tried-- and I was sort of a goody-goody girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heniek, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know of. Um, he was being prepared for bar mitzvah. I remember a rebbe came to our house to teach him. And I was sitting by and listening and trying to learn also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the years difference between you and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years, two years. He was 13, and I was 15 when that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: A private school for Jewish children. It was called a Jewish gymnasium. But it had, uh, classes from first grade to sixth. And I was-- I started from first grade. We were-- spoken language was Polish. But we were taught Hebrew. And some of, uh, Tanakh in-- in-- not in Hebrew, in Polish-- stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what is Tanakh in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, it's the Bible, the Old Testament, the Bible. And I remember starting Latin in the sixth grade, beginning of sixth-- actually, in fifth grade, we started. And Hebrew was taught. English, I studied privately, just before the break of the war a little bit. And that was a normal life of a normal childhood until '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have-- do you remember on-- or your other relatives, on-- your par-- your grandparents? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I had-- my mother's relative lived in Warsaw. My mother's youngest sister was a very pretty-- she was 11 years older than I was. So when I was 12, it made her 23? Uh, she was very beautiful. And I had her picture. And I remember in school, the girls, we collected pictures of artists, of actors. And the girls, my friends in school, want-- thought that she's an actress. And they wanted to exchange pictures with me. That aunt of mine, when we used to come to Warsaw, she introduced me to opera. I was-- the first time when I was 12, I saw in Warsaw. And it made a tremendous impression on me, especially, I remember, as today, the garden where Marguerite is meeting with Faust. And I remember-- Faust-- with Faust. And I remember Mephistopheles coming out from the flames on the floor of the stage. I guess I was so impressed that from then on, I was always interested in oper-- in opera. And I'm an opera lover even now. So there you go. Some memories from childhood that remain with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: My grandfather and grandmother, I remember vaguely. What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, I don't even remember the names, except that they were Kotz family names. Um, because last time I saw them was maybe when I was 11, 12. And we didn't go often there. We probably couldn't afford it. But I remember visiting Warsaw with my younger-- and she took care of me. She was very proud of me. I remember her wedding, my youngest aunt's wedding. And we, uh, were driven-- she and her bridegroom in a [POLISH], which is a horse-pulled wagon, the black, beautiful carriages. I was sitting next to her. She was in a white dress. I was so proud. And at the wedding, she gave me the rose from-- from, uh, the cake. And those memories linger on. But many things I have forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, uh, kind of other things, uh, did you do at free time, when you were not, uh, in your classroom, in class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meeting with my friends outside, playing, going from-- visiting their home, playing in their yards, reading a lot, after we did our homework, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did you like reading?

speaker: interviewee
text: Novels, especially about people-- women who became teachers. Here's another thing that I was born with. I always loved children. Oh, I love little children. I always got, uh, lost. Walking in the park, finding a small child, walking after the child, playing with the child. I took care of the little children in the neighborhood. Always was drawn to young children, which was an indication for the future. I became a nursery school teacher. [SNIFFS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- what kind of things did you do with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: If we didn't quarrel, we had separate activities. So we-- he played with his friends. Or we played outside together with mutual friends. He was only three years older-- younger. And basically, helping him a little bit with homework. He wasn't so eager to study. So we had our moments. But I remember walking with him together, holding hands in the park with my father. He used to take us. My mother was, uh, more preparing house, a housekeeper. But she was looking at us from the window. And we were both dressed very prettily, I remember. She used to make us clothes from the same fabric. He was wearing shorts. I was wearing a skirt, blue and white tops. And she always waved to us from the window, so proud of us. And Daddy took us to places like, uh, sweet shops to eat ice cream and cake. I remember, my parents gave me some money for every day after school. We stopped at a, um, cake shop [SNIFFS] and bought Napoleons or some layer cakes for 15 cents or whatever it was in Polish money. Those are sweet memories from childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a housekeeper, basically. She did not have profession-- a profession. She was keeping the house was very clean and neat, a very good housekeeper, and, um, taking care of her clothes, of, uh, linen, uh, always keeping the house very clean, neat, cooking. That's-- that's what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you gone on those once-a-year services with your parents, too? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Services? Once a year? Yeah, only with my father for Yom-- Yom Kippur, I think. I was playing outside the synagogue. And Daddy was inside. But he didn't observe much. And, uh, when we came back, he met with some of his cousins. And they played cards even on Yom Kippur, I remember. Later, when I was a little older, and I saw my father's family was very, very observant, very religious. In fact, I remember showing-- somebody showing me a picture of my daddy when he was around 12, 11. He was wearing peyos, payot-- those are the ear locks-- and long coat. And then when I got older, I wanted to know more about the family. So I understood from his sister's life, because I visited them, that they are very observant. They lived in the same city. And when I asked-- I-- I don't remember where I got the information. But obviously, they told me. And when my father was 12, his both parents died. I don't know how. And obviously, I understand now that it turned his observance into rebellion. And he dropped everything while they continued.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew about keeping kosher from my aunts, because when they came to our house-- very seldom. My mother was not well at certain times. And they came to see her. And they didn't eat anything in our house. So I learned why. And I used to visit their homes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I had a family-- a member of my family, my mother's sister, who lived in Czestochowa, was the secretary in the school to which I attended. And she was a different type. She was educated. She had her hair blonde. She lived in a very pretty, uh, very well-furnished apartment with those fancy little things that I was not used to seeing in my home. And she always took care of my figure. She used to tell me, pull in your stomach. Stand straight. And that was a different kind of, uh, person. So from very early childhood, I observed two different families and their lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was the synagogue from your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very far. It was in the same neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, can you describe what the synagogue looked like from what you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only outside, I don't remember anything outside. There was-- the synagogue that my father attended, it was like, uh, a neighborhood shtiebel. I don't know how to explain it. And like a small building, another very fancy synagogue that somebody probably donated, and we davened there. But Czestochowa had a large synagogue, which was burned when the Germans came. And that was a beautiful structure. Very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you gone to that synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only saw it from outside. I was never inside. So I cannot describe. But it was very imposing, very elegant, specially built as a synagogue. That I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, can you tell us, uh, how many synagogues were there in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Except for that large one that I remember, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was one in your neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. My neighborhood was very Jewish. And probably there was more than one, but I-- I-- I never-- like little apartments that were designated as a meeting place for prayers, and not a specially built synagogue. But I don't know how many there were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you made-- did you meet or made friends with, uh, children who were not Jewish? Did you have some contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We had-- we saw them going to school. And we used to go to school in groups. Uh, probably-- I never-- as a child, was never exposed to direct anti-Semitism. Nobody threw stones at me that I remember or things like that. But I recall going to school together in groups because of-- probably our parents was-- were afraid that something might happen. But it never happened. And we walked freely on the streets in the evenings. And I remember, though, some incident in front of our house that there was some fight between a Jew, a non-Jew, and screamings, and somebody hurt someone or-- I don't remember exactly what it was. So obviously, there was some friction. But we never had a Polish, uh, friend. In that area where I lived, there were none-- no Polish children that we played with. And at school where I attended, too, were only Jewish children. So we were sort of protected.

speaker: interviewer
text: So school was also in-- in your area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the school was far, far. I told you we walked, uh, about 45 minutes to school. It was in a very elegant area of Czestochowa where the neighborhood was-- it's probably a district where there were offices and schools. I didn't see many-- yeah, people lived there, but for a-- upper class.

speaker: interviewer
text: Upper Jewish class or upper--

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have to leave your neighborhood to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, definitely. We went through many, many different areas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go, then, through the non-Jewish neighborhood on your way to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. They were mixed. The non-Jewish population that I know, lived-- that lived around the ghetto, were also very poor. But the upper class neighborhood, I was not familiar with. I didn't know them personally. But there were Jewish people all over the city and gentiles, too, probably. But I didn't know them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, can you tell me how far did you go with your education?

speaker: interviewee
text: We reached the sixth grade, because I remember I was 12 when the Germans occupied the city. When-- in '39, when they came, some of the first things that they did is closing Jewish schools, so that in sixth grade, my school was closed. But we continued going to our teachers privately, to their homes, to study for a while, until times became such that it was impossible anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll be coming, uh, soon to the, uh, end of the tape. So we will continue with the story on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask--
